chunk_summary: This section discusses structured clinical nutrition assessment beyond anthropometry, emphasizing laboratory test selection, pitfalls in interpreting serum albumin, and a tiered diagnostic approach for growth faltering using WHO/IAP charts.
learning_objectives: Understand the limitations of serum albumin and prealbumin as nutrition markers in the context of inflammation and illness.
Learn a tiered approach to laboratory testing for growth faltering based on clinical presentation and red flags.
Recognize appropriate diagnostic steps for coeliac disease and thyroid dysfunction in children with growth issues.
key_facts: Serum albumin is influenced by inflammation, fluid status, hepatic function, and capillary leak, making it unreliable as a standalone nutrition marker.
Tier 1 tests for typical outpatient growth faltering include CBC, urinalysis, and symptom-driven stool tests for chronic diarrhea or blood.
Coeliac disease testing requires total IgA and tTG-IgA while the child is consuming gluten; if IgA is low, use an IgG-based assay.
Tier 2 tests for stunting or systemic features include TSH, free T4, and inflammatory markers (CRP or ESR) when clinically indicated.
Mildly elevated TSH with normal free T4 should prompt reassessment at an appropriate interval and consideration of thyroid antibodies.
reference_quote: Serum albumin is strongly influenced by inflammation, fluid status, hepatic function, and capillary leak, so a low value should not be equated with protein-energy malnutrition severity. (Reference 4)
document_key_topics: Laboratory test interpretation in growth faltering
